results.question: What is the definition of an automated message system under the Electronic Transactions Act?

output.answer: Under the Electronic Transactions Act, an **'automated message system'** is defined as "a computer program or an electronic or other automated means used to initiate an action or respond to data messages or performances in whole or in part, without review or intervention by a natural person of the final contract or of each of the actions carried out by the automated message system" **[LAW-4]**.

results.ground_truth: Under Section 26 of the Electronic Transactions Act, No. 19 of 2006, an 'automated message system' is defined as a computer program or an electronic or other automated means used to initiate an action or respond to data messages or performances in whole or in part, without review or intervention by a natural person each time an action is initiated or a response is generated by the system. [cite: 277]

results.question: How is 'land' defined under the Registration of Documents Ordinance?

output.answer: Under the Registration of Documents Ordinance, the term 'land' is defined to include "things attached to the earth or permanently fastened to anything attached to" the earth **[LAW-2]**.

Short title. 1. This    Ordinance    may    be    cited    as    the    Registration    of    Documents Ordinance.

- Land registries. 2. (1)  There  shall  continue  to  be  in  Colombo  a  land  register  office  for  the purposes of this Ordinance and the Land Registration Ordinances, No. 5  of 1877 and No. 4 of 1889, with branch offices at such places as the Minister may  from  time  to  time  direct,  and  the  land  register  office  and  branch offices established  under  the  land  Registration  Ordinance,  1891, or any enactment  repealed by that Ordinance  shall be  deemed  to  be  duly established under this Ordinance.
- (2) The  business  of  the  land  register  office  and  branch  offices  shall  be conducted by a Registrar-General of Lands (in this Ordinance called the 'Registrar-General'),  a  Deputy  Registrar-General  of  Lands  and  such number of Assistant Registrars-General (in this Ordinance called 'Assistant Registrars-General') and Registrars of Lands (in this Ordinance  called  'Registrars')  as  the  Minister  may  from  time  to  time consider  necessary.  Acting  appointments  may  be  made  as  may  be necessary in the event of the Registrar-General or the Deputy RegistrarGeneral of Lands or any  Assistant Registrar-General or Registrar being ill or incapable of acting or temporarily absent from duty.
- (3) All appointments under this section shall be made, and may at any  time be  revoked,  in  accordance  with  the  law  for  the  time  being  in  force relating  to  the  appointment  of  public  officers:  Provided  that  in  the case of death, illness, incapacity, absence from duty, or other emergency, the Registrar-General may appoint any person to act as a Registrar for not more than thirty days at any one time and may at any time revoke such appointment.
- (4) All appointments under this section shall be notified in the Gazette.
- (5) All  appointments  made  under  the  Land  Registration  Ordinance, 1891, shall be deemed  to have been made under this Ordinance.
- (6) The Registrar-General may, if he thinks fit, exercise all or any of the powers possessed by a Registrar.

results.question: Draft a formal written complaint to the Consumer Affairs Authority on behalf of a consumer who purchased a defective refrigerator, citing the relevant provisions for the Authority to inquire into the matter.

output.answer:
# FORMAL COMPLAINT TO THE CONSUMER AFFAIRS AUTHORITY

## 1. CAPTION
**BEFORE THE CONSUMER AFFAIRS AUTHORITY OF SRI LANKA**

**Complainant:** `[CONSUMER NAME]`, `[ADDRESS]`, `[NIC NUMBER]`

**Respondent:** `[TRADER/MANUFACTURER NAME]`, `[BUSINESS ADDRESS]`

## 2. TITLE OF THE PLEADING
**COMPLAINT UNDER SECTION 13 OF THE CONSUMER AFFAIRS AUTHORITY ACT NO. 09 OF 2003**

## 3. PARTIES
1. The Complainant is `[CONSUMER NAME]`, residing at `[ADDRESS]`, who is a consumer within the meaning of the Consumer Affairs Authority Act.
2. The Respondent is `[TRADER/MANUFACTURER NAME]`, carrying on the business of selling electrical appliances at `[BUSINESS ADDRESS]`.

## 4. JURISDICTION
This Authority is vested with the power to inquire into complaints regarding the sale of goods that do not conform to implied warranties or standards under **[LAW-7]** (Section 13) and **[LAW-2]** (Section 32), and to grant relief under **[LAW-5]** (Section 13(4)).

## 5. MATERIAL FACTS
1. On `[DATE OF PURCHASE]`, the Complainant purchased a refrigerator (Model: `[MODEL NUMBER]`) from the Respondent for the sum of `[AMOUNT]`.
2. At the time of purchase, the Complainant made known to the Respondent the purpose for which the refrigerator was required, creating an implied warranty that the goods would be reasonably fit for such purpose as per **[LAW-2]**.
3. Shortly after purchase, on `[DATE OF DEFECT]`, the refrigerator failed to function, specifically `[DESCRIBE DEFECT, E.G., FAILURE TO COOL]`, rendering it unfit for its intended purpose.
4. The Complainant has attempted to resolve this matter with the Respondent, but the Respondent has failed to rectify the defect or provide a refund.

## 6. CAUSE OF ACTION
1. The goods supplied by the Respondent do not conform to the implied warranty of fitness for purpose as stipulated in **[LAW-2]**.
2. The Respondent is in breach of the standards and specifications required for the sale of goods under **[LAW-7]**.
3. The Complainant is entitled to seek an inquiry and order for compensation or refund under **[LAW-5]**.

## 7. RELIEF / PRAYER
Wherefore, the Complainant respectfully prays that the Authority:
1. Conduct an inquiry into this complaint as provided for under **[LAW-7]** (Section 13(3)).
2. Order the Respondent to replace the defective refrigerator or refund the purchase price of `[AMOUNT]` as per **[LAW-5]** (Section 13(4)).
3. Grant such other relief as this Authority deems fit.

## 8. VERIFICATION
I, `[CONSUMER NAME]`, do hereby declare that the facts stated above are true and accurate to the best of my knowledge.

Date: `[DATE]`
Place: `[LOCATION]`

## 9. SCHEDULE
1. Copy of Purchase Receipt/Invoice dated `[DATE]`.
2. Warranty Card (if applicable).
3. Correspondence with the Respondent (if any).

## 10. SIGNATURE

__________________________
`[SIGNATURE OF COMPLAINANT]`

## Notes for Completion
- Please ensure the complaint is filed within three months of the sale as required by **[LAW-7]** (Section 13(2)).
- Attach copies of all relevant documents listed in the Schedule.
- Ensure the NIC number and full addresses are provided for both parties.

results.question: Draft a boilerplate clause for a commercial supply agreement that explicitly recognizes the legal validity of electronic signatures and contracts formed via automated message systems, in accordance with the Electronic Transactions Act.

output.answer:
# Commercial Supply Agreement: Electronic Provisions

## 1. Parties
This Agreement is entered into by and between `[PARTY A NAME]`, having its registered office at `[ADDRESS A]`, and `[PARTY B NAME]`, having its registered office at `[ADDRESS B]`, on this `[DATE]` day of `[MONTH]`, `[YEAR]`.

## 2. Recitals
WHEREAS, the Parties intend to enter into a commercial supply arrangement; and
WHEREAS, the Parties desire to utilize electronic means for the formation, execution, and management of this Agreement.

## 3. Definitions
- **"Electronic Communication"** shall have the meaning ascribed to it under the Electronic Transactions Act No. 19 of 2006 (as amended).
- **"Automated Message System"** refers to a computer program or an electronic or other automated means used to initiate an action or respond to data messages or performances in whole or in part, without review or intervention by a natural person.

## 4. Operative Clauses: Electronic Validity
4.1. **Validity of Electronic Contracts:** In accordance with the Electronic Transactions Act, this Agreement and any subsequent amendments or purchase orders shall not be denied legal validity or enforceability solely on the ground that they are in electronic form or were formed through electronic communications **[LAW-2]**, **[LAW-4]**.

4.2. **Automated Systems:** The Parties acknowledge and agree that a contract formed by the interaction of an automated message system and a natural person, or by the interaction of automated message systems, shall not be denied validity or enforceability solely on the ground that there was no review or intervention by a natural person of the final contract or of each of the actions carried out by the automated message system **[LAW-6]**.

4.3. **Electronic Signatures:** Where this Agreement or any related document requires a signature, the Parties agree that an electronic signature, as recognized under the relevant regulatory framework, shall be sufficient to satisfy such requirement.

## 5. Representations and Warranties
Each party represents that it has the necessary authority to enter into this agreement via electronic means and that any electronic record transmitted by its authorized personnel or automated systems shall be binding upon it.

## 6. Termination
This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon `[NUMBER]` days' written notice, which may be delivered via electronic mail to the designated contact addresses of the parties.

## 7. Dispute Resolution
This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Any disputes arising out of this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of `[CITY/DISTRICT]`.

## 8. General Provisions
This document constitutes the entire agreement between the parties. No amendment shall be valid unless made in writing and signed by authorized representatives of both parties.

## 9. Execution
IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement as of the date first written above.

__________________________
For and on behalf of `[PARTY A NAME]`

__________________________
For and on behalf of `[PARTY B NAME]`

## Notes for Completion
- Please insert the specific legal names and addresses for `[PARTY A NAME]` and `[PARTY B NAME]`.
- Ensure the `[DATE]` and `[CITY/DISTRICT]` fields are populated.
- Confirm the notice period in Section 6 matches your commercial requirements.
- Ensure that any specific electronic signature platforms used comply with the standards set by the relevant Certification Authority in Sri Lanka.

results.question: Kamal verbally agrees to sell his commercial property to Nimal for Rs. 50 Million. Nimal pays a Rs. 5 Million advance, and they shake hands on the deal in front of several witnesses. A month later, Kamal changes his mind, refuses to transfer the property, and refuses to return the advance. Nimal wishes to sue Kamal to force the sale of the property. Based on the formal requirements of the law, what legal hurdles does Nimal face in enforcing this contract?

output.answer:
## Issue

Whether a verbal agreement for the sale of immovable property is legally enforceable in Sri Lanka, and whether Nimal can compel Kamal to transfer the property based on such an agreement.

## Rule

Under Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, no sale or transfer of land or other immovable property shall be of force or avail in law unless the same is in writing and signed by the party making the same in the presence of a licensed notary and two or more witnesses present at the same time, with the execution of the deed duly attested by such notary and witnesses **[LAW-5]**. Furthermore, legal precedent establishes that an agreement to retransfer or transfer premises must be notarially attested to be binding **[LAW-8]**.

## Application

In the present scenario, the agreement between Kamal and Nimal is purely verbal. The law explicitly requires that any transfer of immovable property must be executed through a written instrument, signed by the transferor, and attested by a licensed notary and two witnesses **[LAW-5]**. 

Nimal faces a significant legal hurdle: because the contract for the sale of the commercial property was not reduced to writing and notarially attested, it lacks the formal requirements mandated by the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance. Consequently, the agreement is not of force or avail in law **[LAW-5]**. The fact that there were witnesses to the handshake or that an advance payment was made does not cure the defect of failing to comply with the mandatory statutory formalities for the conveyance of immovable property **[LAW-5, LAW-8]**.

## Conclusion

Nimal faces a high probability of failure in an action to force the sale of the property. The verbal agreement is legally unenforceable due to non-compliance with the mandatory requirements of Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance **[LAW-5]**. As the contract is not of force or avail in law, the court cannot compel Kamal to perform the transfer. Nimal's claim for specific performance is likely to be dismissed on the basis that the agreement fails to meet the necessary statutory formalities **[LAW-8]**.

Sale  of  arrack  farm-Agreement  to  sell-Ordinance No.  11  of  1896, s. 11Movables -Tangible  or corporeal  things-Goods-Interpretation.

An  agreement  between  A  and  B  for  the  sale  of  the  right  to  sell  arrack within a defined district and for a given period, which A  had  obtained from the  Government  as  its "  renter, " is  good and  valid although  not  in writing.

It is  not a contract  for  the sale  of "  goods " within  the  meaning of section i of "  The Sale of Goods  Ordinance,  1896. "

The word " movables " as used in the definition for "  goods " does not include anything more than corporeal movables, and the term goods " in section 59 of that Ordinance  means all tangible movable property, except money  and  in  certain  cases crops and  things  attached  to the  soil.

T HE  defendant,  who  had  bought  from  the  Government  the  rent of  the  arrack  farm  of  the  town and  district  of  Negombo  for 1902 and 1903, agreed verbally to sell to the plaintiff the said rent. The  plaintiff complained of a  breach of this agreement by the  defendant  and  claimed  damages.

The defendant took the objection-  that the agreement was not enforceable by  action, inasmuch  as  it  was a  contract for  the sale of  "  goods,"  and  there  was  no  note  o f -  memorandum  of  it  in  writing as required by sub-section 1 of section 4 of Ordinance No. 11 of  1896.

The  'District^ Judge over-ruled  this  objection.

*,

The  defendant appealed.

Dornhorst, K.C., for  defendant, appellant.-The  subject-matter  of the agreement  not  being immovable  properly  must  necessarily fall into the class  "  movable property,  " arid the agreement there­ fore  required  to  be  in  writing under  the Sale  of  Goods  Ordinance.

A  chose  in  action  was  expressly  excluded  from  what  was  comprehended  in  the  term  " goods  "  used  in  the  English  Sale  of  Goods Act,  and  the  District  Judge  was  therefore  wrong  in  applying  to  the present case  the law as to choses in  action under the English Act. The present case was governed by Ordinance  No. 11 of 1896, and  in  it " goods  "  were  said  to  include all "  movables.

48. (1) Where,  under  a  contract  of sale, the  property  in  the  goods  has  passed  to  the buyer,  and  the  buyer  wrongfully  neglects  or refuses to pay for the goods  according to the terms of the contract, the seller may maintain  an  action  against  him  for  the  price of the goods.

(2)  Where,  under  a  contract  of  sale,  the price is payable on a day certain irrespective of delivery, and the buyer wrongfully neglects  or  refuses  to  pay  such  price,  the seller  may  maintain  an  action for the  price, although  the  property  in  the  goods  has  not passed and the goods have not been appropriated to the contract.

49. (1)  Where the buyer wrongfully neglects or refuses to  accept and  pay for the goods,  the  seller  may  maintain  an  action against him for damages for non-acceptance.

(2)  The measure of damages is the estimated loss directly and naturally resulting,  in  the  ordinary  course  of events, from the buyer's breach of contract.

(3)  Where there is an available market for the goods in question the measure of damages is  prima facie  to  be  ascertained  by the  difference  between  the  contract  price and  the  market  or  current  price  at  the  time or times when  the  goods  ought to  have  been accepted, or, if no time was fixed for acceptance,  then  at  the  time  of the  refusal  to accept.

results.ground_truth:
Issue: Whether a verbal agreement for the sale of immovable property is legally enforceable for specific performance.

Rule: Under Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, no promise, bargain, contract, or agreement for establishing any security, interest, or encumbrance affecting land or other immovable property shall be of force or avail in law unless it is in writing and signed by the party making it (or by a person lawfully authorized) in the presence of a licensed notary public and two or more credible witnesses, and duly attested.

Application: The agreement between Kamal and Nimal for the sale of the commercial property was entirely verbal. Despite the payment of an advance and the presence of witnesses, the strict formal requirement of a notarial execution was not met. Therefore, the contract is statutorily void and of no force or avail in law.

Conclusion: Nimal cannot legally enforce the sale of the property (specific performance) because the verbal contract violates the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance. (Note: While Nimal may have a separate quasi-contractual claim in unjust enrichment to recover the advance, the contract of sale itself is unenforceable.)

results.question: A consumer hires a logistics company to transport valuable fragile goods. The standard form contract provided by the company contains a clause stating: 'The company accepts absolute zero liability for any loss or damage to goods in transit, even if caused by the direct negligence of our employees.' The goods are destroyed because the driver negligently crashed the truck while texting. The company points to the contract clause to deny the claim. How should a court evaluate the validity of this exclusion clause?

output.answer:
## Issue

Whether a contract term in a standard form agreement that purports to exclude all liability for loss or damage to goods caused by the negligence of a company's employees is legally valid under the Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997.

## Rule

Under the Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997:

1. **Liability for Negligence (General)**: A contract term that purports to exclude or restrict liability for loss or damage resulting from negligence (other than death or personal injury) shall have effect only in so far as such term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness **[LAW-7]**.

2. **Standard Terms of Business**: Where a party deals on the other's written standard terms of business, the party in breach cannot exclude or restrict any liability in respect of that breach, except in so far as the contract term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness **[LAW-2]**, **[LAW-6]**, **[LAW-8]**.

3. **Acceptance of Risk**: A person's agreement to a contract term or awareness of a notice purporting to exclude or restrict liability for negligence shall not itself be taken as constituting their acceptance of any risk **[LAW-7]**.

## Application

In the present scenario, the logistics company is relying on a clause in its standard form contract to exclude "absolute zero liability" for damage caused by the negligence of its employees. 

First, because the contract is a standard form agreement, the provisions regarding the exclusion of liability for breach of contract apply **[LAW-2]**, **[LAW-8]**. Second, as the damage resulted from the negligence of the company's driver, the clause falls under the scope of the Act's regulation of negligence liability **[LAW-7]**. 

Under the Act, such an exclusion clause is not automatically void, but it is subject to the "requirement of reasonableness" **[LAW-2]**, **[LAW-7]**. The company cannot rely on the consumer's signature or agreement to the contract as an automatic acceptance of the risk of the company's negligence **[LAW-7]**. Therefore, the court must evaluate whether the clause satisfies the test of reasonableness to determine its validity.

## Conclusion

The exclusion clause is not inherently void, but it is subject to the test of reasonableness under the Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997. The court will likely find the clause unenforceable if it fails to meet the requirement of reasonableness. The company cannot rely on the consumer's agreement to the contract as a waiver of the company's liability for negligence. The certainty of this conclusion is high, provided the court determines the clause does not satisfy the reasonableness test.

results.ground_truth:
Issue: Whether a standard contract term excluding liability for property damage caused by negligence is legally valid and enforceable against a consumer.

Rule: Under Section 3(2) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997, a person cannot by reference to any contract term or to a notice given to persons generally or to particular persons exclude or restrict his liability for negligence resulting in loss or damage (other than death or personal injury) except in so far as the term or notice satisfies the 'requirement of reasonableness'. 

Application: The logistics company is attempting to use a blanket exclusion clause in a standard form contract to avoid liability for property damage caused directly by their employee's negligence. The court will not automatically enforce this clause. Instead, the court must subject the clause to the 'requirement of reasonableness'. Given that it is a sweeping exemption in a standard form contract offered to a consumer, it places an unfair burden on the consumer and is highly likely to be deemed unreasonable.

Conclusion: The exclusion clause is not automatically void, but it will only be effective if it passes the reasonableness test. Under these facts, a court is highly likely to strike down the clause as unreasonable, thereby holding the logistics company liable for the negligently destroyed goods.

results.question: How does the Unfair Contract Terms Act differentiate between contract terms that exclude liability for negligence resulting in death or personal injury, versus those excluding liability for other types of loss or damage, and what legal standard must the latter satisfy?

output.answer:
## Executive Summary

The Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997, establishes strict limitations on the ability of parties to exclude or restrict civil liability for negligence. The Act distinguishes between liability for death or personal injury and liability for other forms of loss or damage. While the former is rendered entirely ineffective by statute, the latter is subject to a "reasonableness" test [LAW-1], [LAW-2].

## Findings

### Exclusion of Liability for Death or Personal Injury
Under Section 3(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act, any contract term or notice that purports to exclude or restrict, or has the effect of excluding or restricting, a person's liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence is of no effect [LAW-1], [LAW-2]. This provision applies to business liability [LAW-1]. Furthermore, a person's agreement to such a term or their awareness of such a notice does not constitute acceptance of the risk [LAW-1], [LAW-2].

### Exclusion of Liability for Other Loss or Damage
For loss or damage other than death or personal injury resulting from negligence, the Act provides a more flexible standard. Section 3(2) stipulates that a contract term or notice purporting to exclude or restrict such liability shall have effect only in so far as it satisfies the "requirement of reasonableness" [LAW-1], [LAW-2]. 

### The 'Reasonableness' Test
While the Act mandates that terms excluding liability for non-personal injury loss must satisfy the requirement of reasonableness, the provided source documents do not explicitly define the specific criteria or factors that constitute the "reasonableness" test. The sources confirm that this test applies to business liability [LAW-1] and provide an illustration regarding indemnity clauses, noting that a term requiring a party to indemnify another against damage to third parties resulting from negligence is valid only if it satisfies the test of reasonableness [LAW-6].

## Conclusion

The Unfair Contract Terms Act creates a binary framework for negligence-based liability exclusions. Liability for death or personal injury cannot be excluded under any circumstances [LAW-1]. Conversely, liability for other forms of loss or damage may be excluded or restricted, provided the term or notice meets the statutory requirement of reasonableness [LAW-1], [LAW-2]. The specific legal criteria for determining "reasonableness" are not detailed within the provided source texts.

results.ground_truth: Under Section 3(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act No. 26 of 1997, any contract term or notice that purports to exclude or restrict liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence is absolutely void and of no effect. However, under Section 3(2), a contract term or notice purporting to exclude or restrict liability for any other loss or damage resulting from negligence is not automatically void. Instead, it will only have legal effect in so far as such term or notice satisfies the 'requirement of reasonableness'.

results.question: Under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance (as amended by Act No. 4 of 2024), what are the formal execution requirements for a transfer deed of immovable property, and how does the law accommodate a transferee who is a corporate body unable to be present?

output.answer:
## Executive Summary

Under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, as amended by Act No. 4 of 2024, the transfer of immovable property requires a written deed signed by the parties in the presence of a licensed notary and two witnesses. Recent amendments mandate the inclusion of thumb impressions for both transferors and transferees. Specific provisions have been introduced to accommodate corporate bodies that are unable to be physically present during execution [LAW-7], [LAW-9].

## Findings

### Formal Execution Requirements for Transfer Deeds

Pursuant to the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance (as amended), any sale, transfer, or assignment of immovable property must be in writing and satisfy the following conditions to be of force or avail in law:

*   **Signatures:** The deed must be signed by every executant (or a person duly authorized by them) in the presence of a licensed notary public and two or more witnesses, all of whom must be present at the same time [LAW-7].
*   **Thumb Impressions:** Both the transferor and the transferee are required to affix their left or right thumb impressions above or beside their signatures on the original, duplicate, and protocol of the deed. If both thumbs are missing, other finger or toe impressions may be used [LAW-7].
*   **Notarial Attestation:** The notary must attest the execution. If a signature or thumb impression cannot be obtained for any reason, the notary must state the reason in the attestation, and the executant must provide an alternative finger or toe impression [LAW-7].
*   **Transferor/Transferee Obligation:** In the specific case of a transfer deed, both the transferor and the transferee are strictly required to affix their signatures and thumb impressions as prescribed [LAW-9].

### Execution by Corporate Bodies

The law provides specific mechanisms for transferees that are corporate bodies unable to be physically present for the execution of a deed:

*   **Authorization:** Where a corporate body is the transferee and its Board of Directors is unable to be present to execute the deed, the Board is empowered to authorize any person in writing to sign the deed on its behalf [LAW-9].
*   **Compliance:** The person so authorized must comply with all the formal requirements set out in subsection (1) of the Ordinance, including the requirements for signatures, thumb impressions, and the presence of a notary and witnesses [LAW-9].

## Conclusion

The legal framework for transferring immovable property in Sri Lanka has been tightened by the 2024 amendments to the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, emphasizing the necessity of biometric (thumb/finger/toe) impressions alongside traditional signatures. Corporate transferees are granted flexibility through written authorization, allowing a designated representative to execute the deed on behalf of the Board of Directors, provided all statutory formalities regarding attestation and presence are strictly observed [LAW-7], [LAW-9].

Since  the enactment  of  the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, transfer  of  immovable  property  can  be  made  only  by  means  of  a notatrial  conveyance.  Thus,  section  2  has  been  framed  to take  within its  scope  all  transactions  relating  to  land  excepting  -

- (a) leases  at  will  or  for  any  period  not  exceeding  one  month, and

- (b) contracts or agreements for the cultivation of paddy-fields or chena  lands  for  any  period  not  exceeding  twelve  months  if the  consideration  for  such  contract  or  agreement  shall  be that  the  cultivator  shall  give  to  the  owner  any  share  of  the crop  or  produce.

The notarial conveyance form the contract of sale and it is by virtue of  the  effect  which  the  law  attributes  to  a  notarial  conveyance  that the  purchaser  obtains  his  right  to  be  placed  in  possession  of  the property.

It  is  to  be  noted  that  section  55  of the  National  Savings  Bank  Act contain express provisions for the passing of title to immovable property on  a  certificate  of  sale  signed  by  the  Bank.  The  title  thus  bestowed by  the  certificate  of  sale  becomes  valid  by  operation  of  law.  It  would be  seen  that  the  requirement  in  section  55  of  the  National  Savings Bank  Act  by  expressly  providing  for  passing  of  title  on  a  certificate of  sale  without  a  notarial  attestation  directly  conflicts  with  section  2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance. Therefore,  it is vital to consider whether these competing considerations effectively harm the objectives of  section  2  of  the  Prevention  of  Frauds  Ordinance.  Insofar  as  this aspect  is  concerned  it  is  noteworthy  that  effectiveness  and  sanctity leading  to  the  finality  of  the  certificate  of  sale  is  secured  by  section 3  of  National  Savings  Bank  Act  with  the  stipulation  to  affix  the  seal of  the  Bank  (which  is  in  the  custody  of  the  Board  of  Directors)  in the  presence  of  two  members  of  the  Board  who  are  mandated  to sign  the  instrument  in  token  of  their  presence.

Finally,  it  is  necessary  to  consider  whether  the  National  Savings Bank  Act  being  a  later  specified  enactment  takes  precedence  over the provisions of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance by the application of  the  principle  ' generalia  specialibus  non  derogant ' in  the  interpre­ tation  of  statutes.  It  would  be  clear  that  upon  an  examination  of  the provisions  of  section  55  of  the  National  Savings  Bank  Act,  that  the legislature has recognized disposition of immovable property otherwise than  by  conveyance  exec'uted  before  a  Notary.

The evidence of Mihindu Ratnayake, Assistant Registrar of Lands Kandy,  Ranaweera clerk of the  Land  Registry,  Kumarihamy clerk of the  High Court Kandy were the official witnesses who had given evi­ dence at the District Court. Their evidence clearly established the fol­ lowing  matters;

- a)  that  L.  B.  Ratnayake,  attorney-at-law  and  notary  public  had ceased to practice as a notary from or about November 1984.
- b)  that  the  disputed  deed  is  said  to  have  been  attested  on  the 19th July  1985  (vide deed 2007,  P3)
- c) that  L.B.  Ratnayake  had  not obtained  a licence  to  practice  as a  notary  for  the  year  1985  and  he  had  not  even  applied  for such  a  licence  as  required  by  section  27(2)  of  the  Notaries Ordinance.  Under  section  30  of  the  said  Ordinance  it  is  an offence for a notary to practice without such licence.
- d)  documents  produced  by  defendant-respondent  V10,  12,  V13 and V14 show that he  had applied for such  licences upto the year 1984. However  the  defendant-respondent  had  been unable to produce such a licence or an application for a licence for the year  1985.
- e)  notary  Ratnayake  had  died  on  25.07.1986. After his  death  all his deed registers and protocols of his deeds have been hand­ ed  over to the  registrar of  lands.

BE it enacted by the Parliament of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka as follows: -

1. (1) This Act may  be cited as the  Companies (Amendment) Act, No. 12 of 2025.

(2) The provisions of this Act other than the provisions of this section shall come into operation on such date as the Minister may appoint by Order published in the Gazette .

results.ground_truth: Under Section 2(1) and 2(2) of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance (as amended by Act No. 4 of 2024), a transfer deed for immovable property must be in writing, signed by every executant and the witnesses in the presence of a licensed notary public, all present at the same time and in the presence of one another. Crucially, both the transferor and transferee must affix their signatures and thumb impressions. If the transferee is a corporate body and its Board of Directors is unable to be present to execute the deed, the Board may authorize in writing any other person to sign and affix their thumb impression on the Board's behalf, provided that person complies with the simultaneous presence and attestation requirements of subsection (1).
